_comment: Per-chart narratives. CI (build_prompt.py -> GitHub Models -> write_narrative.py) overwrites these from live numbers; this committed copy is the fallback so the page always reads well. source = 'fallback' | 'github-models'.

notes.shape: The yield curve displays Treasury yields from 1 month to 30 years, with an upward slope considered normal, reflecting higher yields for longer maturities. An inverted yield curve, where short-term yields exceed long-term yields, has historically preceded every modern US recession by 6-18 months. Currently, the 10-year Treasury yield is 4.38% (due June 2026). The 10-year to 2-year spread (2s10s) is 0.3 points, indicating a positive slope which contrasts with the deepest inversion in the series of -0.2 points experienced during 2022-2024.
notes.signal: The 2s10s spread is a key recession indicator, as an inversion (negative spread) has consistently signaled upcoming recessions. The period from 2022 to 2024 featured an inverted curve, but since then, the spread has re-steepened to positive territory, suggesting a diminished immediate signal of recession risk. The New York Fed's recession probability stands at 31%, a moderate level reflecting some ongoing economic concerns. It is important to note that the yield curve often un-inverts shortly before a recession arrives, complicating timing predictions based solely on this metric.
notes.now: Currently, the mildly positive 10-year to 2-year spread and a 31% recession probability indicate some economic uncertainty but not a definitive near-term recession signal. The un-inversion after a significant inversion suggests the curve is in a transitional phase, warranting continued monitoring without drawing firm conclusions on timing or certainty of recession.
